claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm asking about. His name is Alex and he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And the next important question is: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! And one final question to ensure I have all the information:

**Is Alex in any special circumstances such as being in hospital for an extended period, in care, or living away from home for any reason?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, I'm fostering Alex, and the local authority provides maintenance payments for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that important detail. This is crucial for determining eligibility.

Just to clarify: **Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?** (This would typically be payments made to you to cover the costs of looking after Alex as a foster child.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. The council does pay me for his accommodation and maintenance as I'm fostering him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I've completed the assessment for Alex.

**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: While you meet many of the basic requirements (you live in the UK, Alex lives with you, he's under 16, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him), the specific rules around fostering prevent eligibility in your situation.

The Child Benefit rules state that you can get Child Benefit if you foster a child, **but only as long as the local council is not paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.** Since you've confirmed that the local council does pay you for Alex's accommodation and maintenance as part of your foster care arrangement, this means you don't qualify for Child Benefit for him.

I understand this may be disappointing news, but the maintenance payments you receive from the local authority are intended to support Alex's care instead of Child Benefit.

Is there anything else I can help clarify about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is an 8-year-old child who lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, the claimant is fostering Alex and the local council is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance. According to the Child Benefit rules on fostering, you can get Child Benefit if you foster a child, BUT only as long as the local council is NOT paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. Since the local council IS paying towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering 8-year-old Alex and meets most basic eligibility criteria (lives in UK, child lives with them, no one else claiming). However, because the local council is paying towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit under the fostering rules, which specifically exclude cases where the local authority provides such payments.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is an 8-year-old child who lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, the claimant is fostering Alex and the local council is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance. According to the Child Benefit rules on fostering, you can get Child Benefit if you foster a child, BUT only as long as the local council is NOT paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. Since the local council IS paying towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering 8-year-old Alex and meets most basic eligibility criteria (lives in UK, child lives with them, no one else claiming). However, because the local council is paying towards Alex's accommodation and maintenance, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit under the fostering rules, which specifically exclude cases where the local authority provides such payments.
